text.title: Feature Flags Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every Feature Flags application must enforce. These are HARD rules — implementation expedience does not override them.

rule.statement: Every flag has a named human owner and an expiry date <=180 days from creation. Flags past expiry are auto-flagged for removal.
rule.rationale: Owner-less flags rot; expiry-less flags accumulate forever. Both are the source of feature-flag debt.

rule.statement: Flag evaluation is a pure function of (flag_key, context). No side effects — no DB writes, no remote calls inside the evaluation path beyond the SDK.
rule.rationale: Side-effecting evaluation creates phantom dependencies and makes incident triage harder; pure evaluation is easy to reason about.

rule.statement: Flags are declared in one central registry (file or service). No 'shadow flags' inferred from env vars or feature toggles scattered in code.
rule.rationale: Shadow flags hide from audits; central registry makes ownership and lifecycle observable.

rule.statement: Risky features (payment changes, third-party integration switch, schema migration cutover) ship behind a kill-switch flag with default off + monitored on rollout.
rule.rationale: Without a kill-switch, rollback to disable a feature requires a deploy; with one, a single config flip cuts blast radius in seconds.

rule.statement: When a flag is created, the cleanup PR (removing the flag eval branches and the dead-path) is created in draft status and assigned to the flag owner.
rule.rationale: Cleanup gets forgotten. Pre-creating the PR makes the cleanup work concrete and assigned, not 'someday'.

rule.statement: Flag evaluations may nest at most 2 deep in any code path. Beyond 2, the logic is split into separate functions or replaced with a single composite flag.
rule.rationale: Nested flags create combinatorial test cases; >2 deep becomes untestable in practice.

rule.statement: When preconditions in Applies-If hold and no Skip-If trigger applies, walk the procedure end-to-end and run the validator before declaring done.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory run leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The run leaf maps "preconditions met" to the canonical execution path.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the Applies-If preconditions are not met OR any Skip-If disqualifier holds.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory skip leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The skip leaf needs a real rule so non-applicable inputs route cleanly out instead of producing false-positive verdicts.
